hero.title: Let's Master Arrays & Vectors
hero.subtitle: Learn contiguous memory, index-based access, and dynamic array resizing
hero.accent: Build confidence with array indices, vectors, and traversal logic

sections.title: Introduction
content: Array aur Vector linear data structures hote hain jo multiple elements ko sequential order mein store karte hain.
Inmein har element ek index ke through access kiya ja sakta hai.
Array ka size fixed hota hai aur create hone ke baad change nahi kiya ja sakta.
Vector ek dynamic array hota hai jo runtime par apna size automatically increase ya decrease kar sakta hai.

sections.title: One Line Definition
sections.array: A fixed-size collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations.
sections.vector: A dynamic array provided by C++ STL that automatically resizes itself when needed.

array.title: Train Compartments
array.description: Imagine karo aapne train mein 5 compartments reserve kiye hain. Reservation ke baad compartments ki sankhya fix ho jaati hai.

vector.title: Queue Line
vector.description: Queue mein log aate aur jaate rehte hain. Size dynamically change hoti rehti hai.

sections.title: Why We Learn This?
items: Used in almost every coding question
Required for Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Two Pointers and Binary Search
Vectors solve fixed-size limitations of arrays
Most STL-based DSA problems use vectors

explanation: Array stores data in contiguous memory.

vector.capacityExplanation: Capacity generally doubles when full. Exact growth depends on STL implementation.

items.title: Array Out Of Bounds
items.wrong:
int arr[5];
cout << arr[5];

items.title: Accessing Empty Vector
items.wrong:
vector<int> vec;
cout << vec.back();
items.why: Calling back() on empty vector causes runtime error.

sections.title: When Not To Use Array Or Vector
Interview Questions
items: When Not to Use Arrays or Vectors?

sections.title: Interview Rule
sections.content: If size is fixed use Array. If size changes use Vector. If insertions and deletions are frequent, consider another data structure.

string.title: Let's Master Strings
string.subtitle: Learn how computers store text, access characters using indexes, and manipulate words efficiently

hero.title: Let's Master Strings
hero.subtitle: Learn how computers store text, access characters using indexes, and manipulate words efficiently
hero.accent: Build confidence with character indexes, substrings and pattern logic

sections.title: Introduction
content: Strings ek linear data structure hote hain jo characters ko sequential order mein store karte hain. Inmein har character ek index ke through access kiya ja sakta hai. C++ mein string usually \0 (null character) se end hoti hai (especially char arrays mein). String class (std::string) ek dynamic, mutable container hai jo internally apna size manage karta hai aur automatically resize ho jaata hai jab content change hota hai.
Computer asal mein letters nahi samajhta — wo sirf numbers samajhta hai. Har character ka apna fixed ASCII value hota hai.

ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) ek tarah ka translator hai. Computers sirf numbers (0 aur 1) samajhte hain letters nahi. Isliye, har letter, number, ya symbol (jaise 'A' ya '@') ko computer memory mein ek unique number (value) assign kiya gaya hai, jise ASCII value kehte hain.

items.title: Core Concepts
items.description: Understand character indexing, length, immutability rules, substrings, and traversal patterns.

items.title: Time Complexity
items.description: Learn how concatenation, slicing, search, and traversal affect runtime across languages.

items.title: String APIs
items.description: Practice common operations like find, substr, append, split, compare, and replace.

items.title: Patterns
items.description: Recognize palindrome, anagram, sliding window, and prefix/suffix style questions.

sections.title: One Line Definition
sections.left: A fixed-size sequence of characters stored in contiguous memory, terminated by a null character (\0).
sections.right: A dynamic, mutable sequence of characters provided by STL that automatically resizes when modified.

left.title: Char Array — Roll Number Box on Exam Sheet
left.description: Socho exam ya admit card mein roll number likhne ke liye exactly 6 boxes diye hote hain — ek box mein ek digit. Agar tumhara roll number sirf 4 digit ka hai, to baaki 2 boxes khaali reh jaate hain (waste). Aur agar tumhara roll number 7 digit ka hai, to wo us tray mein fit hi nahi hoga — kahin extra likhna padega, jo allowed nahi hai.
left.diagram:
['2']['3']['1']['9']['\0'][ ] ← 1 box khaali, waste ho gaya

Char array mein characters fixed boxes mein store hote hain — agar string chhoti hai to space waste hoti hai, agar bada hai to overflow ho jaata.

right.title: String (STL) — WhatsApp Message Box
right.description: WhatsApp pe message likhte waqt text box apne aap badhta jaata hai jaise tum type karte ho. Koi fixed limit nahi — jitna likho, box utna grow karta hai.
right.diagram:
"Hi"
↓ (typing more...)
"Hi there"
↓ (typing more...)
"Hi there, how are you?"

items.title: Interview relevance
items.description: Used in almost every coding interview (Anagram, Palindrome, Substring problems) and is the foundation of pattern matching.

items.title: Memory understanding
items.description: Helps understand how memory stores character data, handling null terminators, and the difference between stack and heap strings.

items.title: Advanced Patterns
items.description: Required for Sliding Window, Two Pointers, KMP Algorithm, and Trie implementations.

items.title: Real World Systems
items.description: Most real-world software (search engines, parsing, validation, log analyzers) relies heavily on text and string handling.

sections.title: Internal Working
explanation: Char Array is stored in contiguous memory locations, terminated by a null character (\0).
String (STL) — Dynamic Growth: Capacity generally doubles when full, just like vector.
Exact reallocation and growth behavior depends on the STL library implementation.

tabs.label: Char Array (C-Style)
String (STL)

items.title: Char Array Overflow
items.wrong:
char arr[5] = "Hello"; // size is 5
cout << arr;
items.correct:
char arr[6] = "Hello"; // size is 6 (5 letters + \0)
cout << arr;
items.explanation: "Hello" needs 6 bytes including the null terminator, but the array size is only 5. This causes buffer overflow or undefined behavior.

items.title: Comparing Strings with == in C-style Arrays
items.explanation: Using == compares the memory addresses of the arrays, not their character content. Use strcmp() instead.

items.title: Modifying a String Literal
items.wrong:
char* s = "Hello";
s[0] = 'h'; // runtime crash!
items.correct:
char s[] = "Hello"; // writable array
s[0] = 'h'; // safe
items.explanation: String literals are stored in read-only memory. Modifying them directly leads to segmentation fault or crash.

sections.title: When Not To Use Char Array or String

rows.situation: Frequent insertions or deletions in the middle
Frequent prefix search or autocomplete features
Repeated modification or concatenation in a loop

questions.name: Difference between Char Array and String (STL)?
Why are strings immutable in languages like Python/Java but mutable in C++?
How does substr() work internally — does it create a copy?
What is the time complexity of string concatenation in a loop, and why is it costly?
How would you check if two strings are anagrams?
How does the null terminator ( \0 ) work in C-style strings?

sections.title: Interview Rule
sections.content:
If working with raw character-level memory or legacy C-style code, use Char Array.

If you need flexibility, built-in functions, and safety, use String (STL).

If you need fast prefix matching or dictionary-style word storage, consider a Trie or Hash Map instead.

hashing.title: Let's Master Hashing
hashing.subtitle: Learn how hash functions map keys to values, enable O(1) lookups, and power real-world data structures

hero.title: Let's Master Hashing
hero.subtitle: Learn how hash functions map keys to values, enable O(1) lookups, and power real-world data structures
hero.accent: Use keys and frequencies to reduce repeated scanning

sections.title: Introduction
content: Hashing ek technique hai jo data ko fixed-size values (hash codes) mein convert karti hai, taaki koi bhi element O(1) average time mein find, insert, aur delete kiya ja sake.
Hash Table ek data structure hai jo key-value pairs store karta hai aur internally ek array use karta hai jisme hash function keys ko array indices mein map karta hai.
Computer asal mein array index samajhta hai — wo sirf numbers samajhta hai. Har key ka ek hash value hota hai.

Hash Function ek tarah ka calculator hai. Computers direct strings ko index nahi bana sakte. Isliye, har string, number, ya object ko hash function ek unique number mein convert karta hai, jise hash value kehte hain.

items.title: Core Concepts
items.description: Understand keys, values, hash functions, buckets, collisions, maps, and sets.

items.title: Time Complexity
items.description: Learn average O(1) behavior and why collisions can degrade performance.

items.title: Memory Tradeoffs
items.description: Reason about extra space used by maps, sets, and frequency tables.

items.title: STL Operations
items.description: Practice unordered_map, unordered_set, count, find, insert, and erase.

sections.title: One Line Definition
sections.left: A key-value store where a hash function maps keys to array indices for O(1) average-time insert, lookup, and delete.
sections.right: A collection of unique keys backed by a hash table — no duplicates, O(1) average lookup, no ordering.

left.title: Hash Map — Library Card Catalog
left.description: Socho ek library mein laakhon books hain. Agar tum book ka naam leke dete ho, librarian turant uski shelf location bata deta hai — poori library scan kiye bina. Exactly wahi kaam Hash Map karta hai: key (book name) se value (location) seedha milta hai.
left.diagram:
"Harry Potter" → Shelf A-12, Row 3
"Clean Code" → Shelf B-07, Row 1
"DSA Bible" → Shelf C-22, Row 5

Bas waise hi Hash Map mein keys (book names) fixed slots mein store hote hain — agar key exist karti hai to O(1) mein milti hai, agar nahi to bhi O(1) mein pata chal jaata hai.

right.title: Hash Set — Attendance Register
right.description: Ek teacher ke paas students ki attendance track karni hai — koi duplicate entry nahi chahiye. Hash Set exactly yahi karta hai: ek unique names ka collection jahan check karna instant hai.

items.title: Interview Core
items.description: Used in almost every coding interview (Two Sum, Anagram, Subarray problems) and forms the foundation of lookup optimizations.

items.title: O(1) Efficiency
items.description: Eliminates O(n) scan/search and gives O(1) average time complexity for most storage operations.

items.title: Topic Integration
items.description: Required for visited tracking in Graphs, Sliding Window states, and Two Pointer optimizations.

items.title: Real World Systems
items.description: Most software systems, search engines, databases, caches, and indexing mechanisms rely heavily on hashing.

sections.title: Basic Operations & Time Complexity
functions: mp.find(key) - Returns iterator; mp.end() if not found
mp.count(key) - 1 if exists, 0 if not (for map)

sections.title: Internal Working
explanation: Hash Map holds a contiguous array of buckets.
Hash Set (STL) — Dynamic Growth: Capacity generally doubles when load factor exceeds threshold (usually 1.0).
Rehashing copies all elements to a new, larger array. Exact behavior depends on STL implementation.

tabs.label: Hash Map
Hash Set

items.title: Accidental Key Creation with [ ]
items.wrong: if (mp["key"] == 0) // creates "key" with default value!
items.explanation: Accessing mp["key"] automatically inserts it with default value 0 if not present — use mp.find() or mp.count() to check existence.

items.title: Iterating While Modifying
items.explanation: Erasing elements during iteration corrupts the iterator. Collect keys to delete first, then erase them separately.

items.title: Using Custom Objects as Keys Without Hash Function
items.wrong: unordered_map<pair<int,int>, int> mp; // compile error!
items.correct: map<pair<int,int>, int> mp; // ordered map has default comparison logic
items.explanation: unordered_map needs a hash function for the key type. For custom types, define a custom hasher or use map (ordered) instead.

sections.title: When Not To Use Hash Map or Hash Set

rows.situation: Need range queries (keys between A and B)
Need fast substring/pattern search at scale
Memory is very tight (avoid rehashing overhead)

questions.name: What is the difference between map and unordered_map in C++?
Why is the worst-case time complexity of unordered_map O(n)?
How does a hash function work internally?
What is a collision and how is it handled (chaining vs open addressing)?
How would you find the first non-repeating character in a string?
How does the load factor affect hash table performance?

sections.title: Interview Rule
sections.content:
If you need O(1) average lookup, insert, or delete — use unordered_map or unordered_set.

If you need sorted order or range queries — use map or set instead.

If you need fast prefix matching or dictionary-style word storage — consider a Trie or Hash Map instead.

binarysearch.title: Let's Master Binary Search
binarysearch.subtitle: Learn how divide-and-conquer eliminates half the search space every step — powering O(log n) lookups in sorted data

hero.title: Let's Master Binary Search
hero.subtitle: Learn how divide-and-conquer eliminates half the search space every step — powering O(log n) lookups in sorted data
hero.accent: Cut the search space in half with every decision

sections.title: Introduction
content: Binary Search ek searching algorithm hai jo sirf sorted arrays ya collections par kaam karta hai. Har step par woh middle element dekh ke decide karta hai ki target left half mein hai ya right half mein — aur doosra half eliminate kar deta hai.
Iska sabse bada fayda yeh hai ki har iteration mein search space aadha ho jaata hai — isliye even 1 billion elements mein sirf ~30 comparisons lagte hain.
Computer sirf comparisons samajhta hai — Binary Search is principle ko use karke unnecessary elements skip karta hai bina dekhein.

items.title: Core Concepts
items.description: Understand low, high, mid, sorted order, monotonic conditions, and boundaries.

items.title: Time Complexity
items.description: See why halving the search space gives O(log n) runtime.

items.title: Answer Search
items.description: Apply binary search to minimum feasible value and maximum possible value problems.

items.title: Templates
items.description: Practice lower_bound, upper_bound, and custom predicate templates.

cards.title: Iterative Binary Search
cards.content: A search algorithm on a sorted array that repeatedly halves the search space by comparing the target with the middle element — achieving O(log n) time with O(1) space.

cards.title: Recursive Binary Search
cards.content: Same divide-and-conquer logic implemented via recursive function calls — achieving O(log n) time with O(log n) call-stack space.

cards.title: Binary Search on Answer (Parametric Search)
cards.content: A powerful variant where Binary Search is applied not on an array index, but on a range of possible answers — used when the answer has a monotonic feasibility condition (true...true...false...false or vice versa).

left.title: Iterative — Dictionary Lookup
left.description: Socho tum ek physical dictionary mein word 'Mango' dhundh rahe ho. Kya tum page 1 se start karoge? Nahi! Tum middle open karoge, check karoge — M pehle hai ya baad mein — aur accordingly aadha dictionary ignore kar doge. Exactly yahi Binary Search karta hai.
left.diagram:
Step 1: Open middle of dict
Step 2: M comes after? → search right half
Step 3: Open new middle → word found!

center.title: Recursive — Guess the Number
center.description: 1 to 100 ke beech koi number guess karo. Agar tum 50 bolte ho aur jawab aata hai 'zyada hai' — tum turant 1–50 eliminate kar dete ho aur recursively baaki half (51-100) mein guess karna continue karte ho.
center.diagram:
Target: 73
Guess 50 → 'Greater' → search [51..100]
Guess 75 → 'Smaller' → search [51..74]
Guess 62 → 'Greater' → search [63..74]

right.title: Parametric — Eating Bananas
right.description: Jab tum pooch rahe ho: 'minimum speed kya ho ki Koko k hours mein sab bananas kha sake?' — yeh ek monotonic condition hai (feasible for speed x implies feasible for any speed > x). Binary Search on the answer range karo.
right.diagram:
Feasibility condition:
Speed:  1     2     3     4     5     6
Status: False False True  True  True  True
Target: Find the first True value (3)

items.title: Interview Core
items.description: Used in almost every coding interview — LeetCode has 200+ Binary Search problems, including Aggressive Cows and Book Allocation.

items.title: O(log n) Efficiency
items.description: Eliminates brute-force O(n) searches, making it critical for large-scale systems (even 1 billion elements require just ~30 steps).

items.title: Real World Systems
items.description: Used in databases (B-Trees), OS (page tables), game engines (collision detection), and internal math library functions like sqrt() and pow().

items.title: Parametric Search
items.description: Forms the core of Binary Search on Answer, solving complex 'minimum/maximum feasible value' problems efficiently.

rows.description: Find exact target in sorted array

rows.stl: On match: ans = mid; high = mid - 1
On match: ans = mid; low = mid + 1
ans = mid; high = mid - 1 when >= target
ans = mid; high = mid - 1 when > target
Check which half is sorted, then divide
Track best answer while boundary moves

sections.title: Internal Working

Detail: Set search boundaries to full array
Continue while valid search range exists
Safe mid formula — avoids integer overflow

explanation: Binary Search operates on a step-by-step division of the search space.

Why mid = low + (high-low)/2 and NOT (low+high)/2?: Agar low = 2,000,000,000 aur high = 2,000,000,001 ho toh (low+high) integer overflow kar jaata hai C++/Java mein. low + (high-low)/2 always safe rehta hai.

tabs.label: Iterative (C++)
Recursive (C++)
lower_bound()

items.title: Not Handling Sorted Precondition
items.wrong:
// Applying Binary Search directly
binary_search(unsortedArr.begin(), unsortedArr.end(), target);
items.explanation: Applying Binary Search on an unsorted array gives wrong results with no error. Always verify or sort the array first.

items.title: Using (low + high) / 2 — Integer Overflow
items.wrong: int mid = (low + high) / 2; // overflow risk!
items.correct: int mid = low + (high - low) / 2; // overflow safe
items.explanation: If low and high are very large, their sum can exceed the maximum integer limit. The subtraction-based calculation prevents this.

items.title: Wrong Loop Condition — Misses Last Element
items.explanation: Using strictly less than (<) skips checking when the search space reduces to a single element where low == high.

items.title: Wrong Boundary Update — Causes Infinite Loop
items.wrong:
low = mid; // never moves past mid
high = mid;
items.correct:
low = mid + 1; // always makes progress
high = mid - 1;
items.explanation: Setting boundaries directly to mid without shifting by 1 can result in low and high getting stuck, causing infinite loops.

items.title: Forgetting to Track Answer in First/Last Occurrence
items.wrong: if (arr[mid] == target) return mid; // returns any occurrence
items.explanation: Simply returning mid on a match returns any occurrence. Save the matched index in a separate variable, then adjust boundaries to check the rest of the half.

sections.title: When Not To Use Binary Search

rows.situation: Array is unsorted and sorting cost is too high
Need O(1) average lookup by key
Searching in a Linked List (no random access)
Need all positions of duplicates efficiently

questions.name: What is the prerequisite for Binary Search? Why does it require sorted input?
Why do we use mid = low + (high-low)/2 instead of (low+high)/2?
How do you find the first and last occurrence of a target in a sorted array?
How does Binary Search work on a rotated sorted array? (LeetCode 33)
What is Binary Search on Answer? Give a real problem example.
How would you implement sqrt(n) using Binary Search?
What is the time and space complexity difference between iterative and recursive Binary Search?
How do lower_bound() and upper_bound() work internally?
Can Binary Search work on floating-point ranges? How?
How do you count occurrences of a target in a sorted array in O(log n)?

sections.title: Interview Rule
sections.content:
If you need O(log n) search in sorted data — use Binary Search.

If you need O(1) average lookup by key — use Hash Map instead.

If you see 'minimum/maximum feasible value' or a monotonic condition — think Binary Search on Answer (Parametric Search).

linkedlist.title: Let's Master Linked Lists
linkedlist.subtitle: Learn how nodes chain together with pointers, enable dynamic memory, and power real-world data structures

hero.title: Let's Master Linked Lists
hero.subtitle: Learn how nodes chain together with pointers, enable dynamic memory, and power real-world data structures
hero.accent: Think in references instead of indexes

sections.title: Introduction
content: Linked List ek linear data structure hai jo nodes ki chain hoti hai. Har node mein do cheezein hoti hain: ek data (value) aur ek pointer jo next node ka address store karta hai. Array ki tarah contiguous memory mein store nahi hoti — har node memory mein kahin bhi ho sakti hai.
Computer asal mein memory addresses samajhta hai — wo sirf numbers samajhta hai. Har node ka apna memory address hota hai.
Pointer ek tarah ka address book hai. Computer directly data dhundhta nahi — isliye, har node apne andar next node ka address (pointer) rakhti hai, jise hum link kehte hain.

items.title: Core Concepts
items.description: Understand nodes, head, tail, next pointers, null termination, and traversal.

items.title: Time Complexity
items.description: Compare O(1) pointer insertion with O(n) search and indexed access.

items.title: Pointer Changes
items.description: Practice reconnecting links for insert, delete, reverse, and merge operations.

items.title: Patterns
items.description: Learn fast-slow pointer, cycle detection, reversal, and merge techniques.

cards.title: Singly Linked List
cards.content: A linear chain of nodes where each node holds data and a pointer to the next node — enabling dynamic, non-contiguous memory allocation with O(1) insert/delete at head.

cards.title: Doubly Linked List
cards.content: A linked list where each node holds data, a pointer to the next node, AND a pointer to the previous node — enabling O(1) insert/delete at both head and tail, and bidirectional traversal.

cards.title: Circular Linked List
cards.content: A singly or doubly linked list where the last node's next pointer points back to the head — enabling continuous circular traversal without a null terminator.

left.title: Singly LL — Train Bogies
left.description: Socho ek train hai jisme bogies (compartments) ek dusre se judi hain. Har bogie ke andar passengers hain (data), aur har bogie ko pata hai ki aage kaun si bogie hai (next pointer). Lekin peechhe kaun si thi? Woh nahi pata — sirf aage dekhti hai.
left.diagram:
[Engine] → [Bogie A] → [Bogie B] → [Bogie C] → NULL

Singly Linked List mein har node sirf next node ko jaanti hai. Peeche jaana possible nahi bina poori list traverse kiye.

center.title: Doubly LL — Browser History
center.description: Browser tab mein Back aur Forward dono kaam karte hain. Har page ko pata hai ki pehle kaun sa tha aur agle mein kaun aayega — exactly Doubly Linked List ki tarah, prev aur next references ke saath.

right.title: Circular LL — Music Playlist Loop
right.description: Jab playlist repeat mode on hota hai — last song khatam hone ke baad pehla song phir se chalta hai. Circular Linked List exactly yahi karta hai: last node ka next pointer head ko point karta hai.

items.title: Pointer Manipulation
items.description: Helps build deep pointer clarity, dynamic memory reallocation, and structural link modifications.

items.title: Interview Standards
items.description: Used in almost every coding interview (Reverse List, Detect Cycle, Merge Lists, and Floyd's Cycle detection).

items.title: Core Foundations
items.description: Forms the foundation of Stacks, Queues, Deques, Graph adjacency lists, LRU Cache, and Memory Allocators.

items.title: Dynamic Growth
items.description: Eliminates the fixed-size constraints of arrays, allowing the structure to dynamically shrink and grow.

rows.stl: Updates head pointer to new node
Traverses to end (or uses tail ptr) and links
Bypasses first node and frees memory
O(n) for Singly LL; O(1) for Doubly LL with tail ptr

rows.description: Find and remove node by value
rows.stl: Bypasses targeted node by connecting prev to next
Returns node pointer or nullptr if not found

sections.title: Internal Working
explanation: Linked List nodes are stored in non-contiguous memory locations (e.g. 1000, 2200, 3100), linked together via pointer addresses.
Doubly Linked List: Each node contains three fields: prev pointer | data | next pointer. This doubles pointer memory overhead but enables O(1) bidirectional scans and direct node deletions.

formula.label: Memory Layout
formula.example: Non-contiguous heap-allocated nodes connected via memory address references

tabs.label: Singly Linked List
Doubly Linked List

items.title: Not Handling Empty List (nullptr Dereference)
items.explanation: Attempting to access head->next when head is nullptr triggers a segmentation fault or undefined behavior. Always assert if head is valid first.

items.title: Losing Node Reference (Memory Leak)
items.explanation: Advancing the head pointer directly before saving it leaves the original head node dangling in heap memory, causing a leak.

items.title: Forgetting to Update Tail Pointer in Doubly LL
items.explanation: When inserting the very first node into a Doubly Linked List, both head AND tail must point to it. Forgetting to set the tail pointer leaves it null and breaks tail updates.

sections.title: When Not To Use Linked List

rows.situation: Need O(1) random access by index

questions.name: What is the difference between Singly and Doubly Linked List?
How do you detect a cycle in a linked list? (Floyd's Algorithm)
How do you reverse a linked list in-place?
What is the time complexity of inserting at the middle of a linked list?
How would you find the middle node of a linked list in one pass?
How does a linked list differ from an array in terms of memory layout?

sections.title: Interview Rule
sections.content:
If you need O(1) insert/delete at head or tail — use Linked List.

If you need O(1) random access by index — use Array or Vector instead.

If you need bidirectional traversal or O(1) deletion given a node pointer — use Doubly Linked List.

stack.title: Let's Master Stack
stack.subtitle: Learn LIFO processing, monotonic stacks and expression handling

hero.title: Let's Master Stack
hero.subtitle: Learn LIFO processing, monotonic stacks and expression handling
hero.accent: Use last-in-first-out order to manage pending work

sections.title: Introduction
content: Stack ek linear data structure hai jo LIFO (Last In First Out) principle follow karta hai.
Iska matlab hai ki jo element sabse last mein insert (Push) kiya gaya hoga, wahi sabse pehle remove (Pop) hoga.
Stack mein insertion aur deletion dono operations sirf ek hi end se perform hote hain, jise Top kaha jata hai.
Jab koi naya element add kiya jata hai to woh Top par add hota hai aur jab koi element remove kiya jata hai to woh bhi Top se hi remove hota hai.

items.title: Core Concepts
items.description: Understand push, pop, top, empty state, and LIFO behavior.

items.title: Time Complexity
items.description: Learn why standard stack operations are O(1).

items.title: Monotonic Stack
items.description: Use stack ordering to solve next greater, histogram, and span problems.

items.title: STL Operations
items.description: Practice stack, vector as stack, push, pop, top, size, and empty.

sections.title: One Line Definition
sections.content: Stack ek aisa data structure hai jisme insertion aur deletion sirf ek side se hoti hai, jise Top kehte hain.

sections.title: Real Life Example
content: Socho tumhare paas plates ka stack hai.
Nayi plate hamesha upar rakhi jaati hai.
Plate hamesha upar se hi nikali jaati hai.
Jo plate sabse last me rakhi gayi thi, wahi sabse pehle niklegi.
Yahi Stack ka concept hai.

items.title: Why Stack?
items.description: Programming me kai situations aisi hoti hain jahan hume sabse recently added item ko pehle access karna hota hai. Normal array ya queue is behavior ko efficiently provide nahi karte.

items.title: Core Benefits
items.description: Stack hume data ko organized way me store karne, recent operations ko quick access dene, aur recursion/function calls manage karne me help karta hai.

items.title: Where We Use Stack
items.description: Undo / Redo Feature, Browser History maintenance, Function Calls management, Recursion, Parentheses Matching, DFS Traversal, Next Greater Element, and Monotonic Stack Problems.

items.title: Real Applications
items.description: Function Calls & Recursion, Browser History, Undo / Redo Feature, Expression Evaluation, Parentheses Matching, DFS Traversal, and Backtracking Algorithms.

rows.description: Top par element insert karta hai
Stack empty hai ya nahi check karta hai

sections.title: Internal Working
explanation: Stack initialization: Stack <int> name; creates empty indexes.
Elements memory me continuously store hote hain (Address 1000, 1004, 1008).

tabs.label: C++
tabs.code:
stack<int> st;

st.push(10); // insert the element
st.pop(); // remove the top element
cout << st.top(); //return the top element

tabs.label: Java
tabs.code:
Stack<Integer> st = new Stack<>();

st.push(10); // insert the element
st.pop(); // remove the top element
System.out.println(st.peek()); //return the top element

tabs.label: Python
tabs.code:
st = []

st.append(10) # insert the element
st.pop() # remove the top element
print(st[-1]) #return the top element

items.title: Ignoring edge cases
items.wrong:
// assumes input is always valid
solve(data);
items.explanation: Empty input, one element input, duplicates, and boundary positions should be checked before finalizing a solution.

items.title: Using brute force when a pattern exists
items.explanation: The demo docs intentionally show where you can replace generic advice with topic-specific mistakes.

queue.title: Let's Master Queue
queue.subtitle: Learn FIFO processing, deque operations and BFS foundations

hero.title: Let's Master Queue
hero.subtitle: Learn FIFO processing, deque operations and BFS foundations
hero.accent: Process items in the same order they arrive

sections.title: Introduction
content: A queue is a linear data structure where the first inserted element is removed first.
Queues are used for scheduling, buffering, breadth-first search, and level-order traversal.

items.title: Core Concepts
items.description: Understand push, pop, front, back, empty state, and FIFO behavior.

items.title: Time Complexity
items.description: Learn why queue insertion and removal are O(1).

items.title: BFS Flow
items.description: Use queues to process graph and tree levels in order.

items.title: STL Operations
items.description: Practice queue, deque, push, pop, front, back, size, and empty.

sections.title: One Line Definition
sections.content: A queue follows FIFO order, meaning the earliest pushed element is the first one removed.

sections.title: Real Life Example
content: A queue behaves like people waiting for a service counter.
The person who joined first gets served first.

items.title: Interview Relevance
items.description: Queue problems test process ordering and simulation.

items.title: Competitive Programming
items.description: BFS, shortest path in unweighted graphs, and sliding window deque patterns are common.

items.title: Real World Applications
items.description: Task schedulers, message brokers, print queues, and buffers use queue behavior.

items.title: Problem Solving Foundation
items.description: Queues make level-order traversal and state expansion easier to reason about.

from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append(10)
q.append(20)
print(q[0])
q.popleft()
